content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Honorable Jeffrey S. Bostwick 
San Diego Superior Court 
PO Box 122724 
San Diego, CA 92112-2724 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-064 
Dear Honorable Judge Bostwick: 
April 28, 2016 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding gift provisions of the Political 
Reform Act (the "Act").1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
QUESTIONS 
I. Is your attendance and your wife's attendance to an award event held for volunteers of 
the Boy Scouts of America (BSA) where evening dinner and entertainment will be 
provided a reportable gift under the Act? 
2. Is your personal friend's attendance to this award event, as your guest, a reportable gift 
under the Act? 
CONCLUSION 
l. No. The hospitality and long term close personal friend exceptions are applicable to the 
invitation extended to you and your wife. 
2. Yes. There are no exceptions applicable to your personal friend's attendance, as your 
guest, to the event. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS 
File No. A-16-064 
Page No. 2 
You are a Superior Court Judge and, therefore, a Form 700 filer. You have been invited to 
attend an award event in June 2016 in association with your volunteer services for the BSA. The 
event will be hosted by your personal friend, whom you've known since 2011, and will take place 
at the host's home. The host will be present at the event. The host is not a California lobbyist nor 
connected in any way to your official position. The invitees consist of BSA volunteers who staffed 
a 2014 BSA national training course, the course participants, spouses, and guests. It is estimated 
that there will be approximately 200 people in attendance. The event, including the catered food and 
entertainment will be paid by the host from his personal funds. You do not know the precise cost of 
the event, but you state that it will far exceed $50. Your invitation to this event is unrelated to your 
official position. 
Act as: 
ANALYSIS 
Subject to the exceptions provided in Regulation 18942, the term gift is defined under the. 
"Any payment2 that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to the extent that 
consideration of equal or greater value is not received and includes a rebate or 
discount in the price of anything of value unless the rebate or discount is made in 
the regular course of business to members of the public without regard to official 
status." (Section 82028(a).) 
Furthermore, a payment provided to or for the use of a family member is a gift to the official 
if"there is no established working, social, or similar relationship between the donor and the family 
member that would suggest an association between the donor and the family member suitable or 
appropriate for making the type of payment provided to the family member." (Regulation 
18943(c)(I ).) 
Under the Act, public officials listed in Section 872003 are subject to reporting obligations 
requiring any gifts received by the official aggregating S50 or more during the calendar year to be 
disclosed on the official's statement of economic interest. (Section 87207(a)( I).) 
Based on the facts presented, the cost of the invitations extended to you, your wife, and your 
guest will exceed $50 and are therefore reportable gifts subject to the Act's disclosure requirements. 
However, the Act provides an exception to the definition of gifts for the cost of home 
hospitality. (Regulation l 8942(a)(7).) Home hospitality is defined, in part, as: 
"Any benefit received by the official and the official's spouse and family members 
when accompanying the official, consisting of food, typical home entertainment, or 
l Section 82044 defines payment, in pertinent part, as any "rendering of ... services or anything else of value, 
whether tangible or intangible." 
l Section 87200 lists, in pertinent part, ''judges". 


File No. A-16-064 
Page No. 3 
occasional overnight lodging provided by an individual with whom the official has 
a relationship, connection or association unrelated to the official's position and the 
hospitality is provided as part of that relationship, connection, or association in the 
individuals home when the individual is present ... Any benefit received, other 
than the use of the premises, by any guests of the official other than the official's 
spouse and family members who are present at the request of the official or the 
official's agent are gifts to the official." (Regulation 18942.2.) 
Based on your facts, the award event will take place at the host's home while the host is 
present. Furthennore, your relationship to the host is unrelated to your official position and the 
hospitality is being provided to you in association with the volunteer services you rendered to the 
BSA. Therefore, the hospitality extended to you and your wife is excluded from the Act's definition 
of "gift." Accordingly, your attendance and your wife's attendance to the event are not subject to 
the reporting requirements.4 
However, the home hospitality payments received by your guest, a personal friend, is a gift 
subject to the reporting requirements because as stated above in Regulation ·I 8942.2 that "[a]ny 
benefit received, other than the use of the premises, by any guests of the official other than the 
official's spouse and family members-who are present at the request of the official or the official's 
agent are gifts to the official." 
Please note that the Act's gift limits do not apply to a person in his or her capacity as judge. 
(Section 89503(d).) Instead, they are subject to judge's gift limits under California Code of Civil 
Procedure Section 170.9. 
LN:jgl 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
«1 
By: 
Lauren Ngo 
Intern, Legal Division 
4 The Act also provides an exception to the definition of gifts for a payment provided to an official by an 
individual with whom the official has a long tenn, close personal friendship unrelated to the official's position with the 
agency, unless the individual providing the benefit to the official is listed under subsections (D)(i-iii).(Regulation 
18942(18)(C).) The individual who is hosting the event is a personal friend whom you have known since 201 l and your 
facts do not indicate that any limitations placed on this exception apply. Therefore, the home hospitality payment 
extended to you and your wife may also be excluded from the Act's definition of"gift" pursuant to the long tenn close 
personal friend exception.

guest, to the event.
